- Robotics and CNC manufacturing: a gantry robot or 3D-printer head executing a 'move to' command performs a translation of the tool position; path planning concatenates translation vectors, and composition-by-addition is why waypoint displacements simply sum.
- Tiling, textiles, and architecture: wallpaper patterns, brick bonds, and printed fabrics repeat by translational symmetry — the design is invariant under translation by lattice vectors, the mathematical mechanism that lets a small motif cover an unbounded surface seamlessly.
- Mapping and GPS coordinate shifts: converting between local map frames displaced from one another (or dead-reckoning a position after a known displacement) is vector translation of coordinates, applied identically to every feature on the map layer.

**Practice progression**

Item types: Apply a given translation vector to points and polygons and plot the images, Recover the translation vector from a pre-image/image pair by coordinate subtraction, then verify on all vertices, Write the coordinate rule (and the inverse rule) for a translation described in words ('4 left and 3 up'), Verify distance preservation on a chosen slanted side using the distance formula, Compose two translations and give the single equivalent translation vector, Decide from a picture whether a motion is a pure translation (checking parallelism of corresponding sides). Suggested item count: 12.

Start with single-point translations and sign handling for negative components (developing); move to full polygons, inverse translations, and vector recovery from figures (developing-to-proficient); progress to isometry verification with the distance formula and composition of two translations (proficient); finish with is-it-a-translation diagnosis problems mixing in near-miss rotations (proficient).

**Assessment objectives**

Formats: Coordinate computation with self-check: translate a triangle and recover the vector from the image, Short constructed response: prove translations preserve distance using coordinate differences, Diagram diagnosis: decide whether a shown motion is a translation and justify with parallelism/orientation, Quick items on inverse and composition of translation vectors. Bloom alignment: Apply — students execute translation rules, recover vectors, and verify invariants on novel figures; the coordinate-difference proof item reaches understanding/analysis of why the isometry property holds..

Mastery signal: The student can translate any figure by any signed vector without sign errors, recover the translation vector from any point-image pair, explain via coordinate differences why distances and slopes are preserved, and state confidently that a nonzero translation fixes no points.

**Teacher notes**

Anchor everything to 'every point gets the identical instruction' — draw the displacement arrow at five or six scattered points, not just at the vertices, before doing any coordinate work. Make the coordinate-difference cancellation proof explicit at least once; it is short enough for this level and inoculates against the belief that isometry claims are just teacher assertions. Watch for the two dominant errors — sign direction (negative components) and slight unintentional rotation when students slide tracing paper — and address both with a verify-one-vertex-first routine.

**Student notes**

A translation is a pure slide: pick the vector (h, k) and add it to every point — right/left by h, up/down by k, no turning, no flipping. Quick self-check: subtract any original vertex from its image; you must get back exactly (h, k) every time.

**Prerequisite graph**

- Requires: math.geom.transformations
- Unlocks (future prerequisite links): none yet mapped
- Cross-topic connections (graph cross-links): none
- Narrative: The translation vector (h, k) is your first working vector, and translation-composition-by-addition is exactly vector addition — the bridge into math.geom.vectors-2d. In linear algebra, translations are the key non-example of a linear map (they move the origin), which motivates homogeneous coordinates in computer graphics, and in functions, the graph shift f(x) → f(x − h) + k is this same transformation applied to a graph.

**Teaching hints — review triggers**

- Student misplaces points with negative coordinates or confuses (x, y) order — review plotting in math.geom.coordinate-plane before translation rules.
- Student cannot compute distances between coordinate points — review the distance formula, since isometry verification depends on it.
- Student errs on signed arithmetic like 2 + (−2) or 1 − 3 — remediate integer addition/subtraction, the direct cause of most translation mistakes.
- Student cannot state what a transformation or isometry is — revisit math.geom.transformations for the function-on-the-plane framing before specializing to translations.

**Spaced repetition / revision guidance**

Redo one complete cycle from memory: translate a triangle by a vector with one negative component, verify a slanted side length before and after, and recover the vector by subtraction. If any sign error appears, drill five single-point translations with negative components before returning to figures.

---

### Rotation

*Concept ID: `math.geom.rotation` · Difficulty: proficient · Bloom level: apply · Mastery threshold: 0.8 · Estimated study time: 4h*

**Learning objective.** Students will be able to rotate points and figures by a given angle about a given center, apply the exact coordinate rules for 90°, 180°, and 270° rotations about the origin and the general rule (x, y) → (x cos θ − y sin θ, x sin θ + y cos θ), verify that rotations preserve distance and orientation, and reduce rotation about an arbitrary center to translate–rotate–translate.

A transformation that turns every point by angle θ about a fixed center; expressed by a 2×2 rotation matrix.

A rotation turns the entire plane by a fixed angle θ about a fixed point called the center: every point travels along a circular arc centered there, sweeping the same angle. First principles again — what is fixed? Exactly one point, the center (for θ not a multiple of 360°), which is the rotation's fingerprint among the isometries. What is preserved? Every distance and every angle, so images are congruent to pre-images; and unlike reflection, orientation is preserved — a counterclockwise-labeled triangle stays counterclockwise. The physical model is a sheet of paper pinned at one point and turned: nothing about the sheet distorts, and only the pin stays put. By convention positive angles rotate counterclockwise.

About the origin, rotations have clean coordinate rules worth deriving rather than memorizing. A 90° counterclockwise rotation sends (x, y) → (−y, x): you can see this by rotating the basis directions — the point (1, 0) must go to (0, 1) and (0, 1) must go to (−1, 0) — and checking that (x, y) = x(1,0) + y(0,1) rides along. Applying the 90° rule twice gives 180°: (x, y) → (−x, −y); three times gives 270° CCW: (x, y) → (y, −x). The general angle uses trigonometry: (x, y) → (x cos θ − y sin θ, x sin θ + y cos θ), which is precisely the 2×2 rotation matrix [[cos θ, −sin θ], [sin θ, cos θ]] acting on the coordinate vector — your first serious meeting with the matrix representation of a geometric operation. Each special rule falls out of the general one (θ = 90° gives cos θ = 0, sin θ = 1, hence (−y, x)), so there is really only one formula.

Rotation about an arbitrary center C is handled by a strategy more important than the formula itself: translate C to the origin, rotate there, translate back. This conjugation pattern — move the problem to where it's easy, solve, move back — recurs across mathematics and engineering. Rotations about a common center compose by adding angles, the inverse of rotating by θ is rotating by −θ, and a 180° rotation is a 'point reflection' through the center — but it is not a mirror reflection, because orientation survives. Ahead, the rotation matrix is the flagship example when transformations unlock math.linalg.linear-map: it is the lens through which linear algebra re-explains all of rotation geometry, and it is the object game engines and robot controllers multiply thousands of times per frame.

**Key ideas**

- A rotation is specified by a center and a signed angle; every point sweeps that angle along a circle about the center, and positive angles are counterclockwise by convention.
- The center is the unique fixed point — the fingerprint distinguishing rotation from translation (no fixed points) and reflection (a fixed line).
- Rotations are orientation-preserving isometries: distances, angles, and the clockwise/counterclockwise labeling sense all survive, so images are congruent and never mirror-flipped.
- Origin rules to derive, not memorize: 90° CCW (x, y) → (−y, x); 180° (x, y) → (−x, −y); 270° CCW (x, y) → (y, −x); each follows from tracking where (1, 0) and (0, 1) land.
- The general rule (x, y) → (x cos θ − y sin θ, x sin θ + y cos θ) is the rotation matrix [[cos θ, −sin θ],[sin θ, cos θ]] in action; all special cases are substitutions into it.
- Rotation about a center other than the origin = translate the center to the origin, rotate, translate back — the move-solve-move-back pattern that recurs throughout mathematics.
- Rotations about the same center compose by adding angles and invert by negating the angle; each point's distance to the center is invariant (points stay on their circles).

**Vocabulary**

- **center of rotation** — the unique fixed point about which the plane turns
- **angle of rotation** — the signed angle every point sweeps; positive means counterclockwise
- **rotation matrix** — the array [[cos θ, −sin θ],[sin θ, cos θ]] whose action on (x, y) performs the rotation about the origin
- **orientation-preserving** — keeping the counterclockwise labeling sense of figures; true of rotations, false of reflections
- **conjugation (translate–rotate–translate)** — the strategy of moving an off-origin center to the origin, rotating, and moving back
- **rotational symmetry of order n** — a figure unchanged by rotation through 360°/n about its center

**Common misconceptions**

- *Misconception:* Rotations always happen about the origin, so the rule (x, y) → (−y, x) can be applied no matter what center the problem states.
  *Correction:* The origin rules are valid only for center (0, 0). For any other center C(a, b), you must conjugate: subtract (a, b), apply the origin rule, add (a, b) back. Rotating (2, 1) by 90° CCW about (1, 1) gives (1, 2) — not (−1, 2), which is what the raw origin rule would wrongly produce.
- *Misconception:* A 180° rotation is the same as a reflection, since both 'flip' the figure to the other side.
  *Correction:* A 180° rotation about the origin sends (x, y) → (−x, −y) and preserves orientation (no mirror image); a reflection over the x-axis sends (x, y) → (x, −y) and reverses orientation. Compare a labeled triangle: after a 180° rotation its vertices still read counterclockwise; after a reflection they read clockwise. Different transformations, different invariants.
- *Misconception:* Positive angles rotate clockwise (or the direction doesn't matter).
  *Correction:* The universal mathematical convention is that positive angles are counterclockwise, matching the unit circle in trigonometry. A 90° rotation and a −90° (i.e., 270°) rotation send (3, 4) to different points: (−4, 3) versus (4, −3). Direction always matters unless the angle is a multiple of 180°.
- *Misconception:* Under a rotation, points farther from the center turn through a bigger angle (or points move 'more' in a way that changes the figure).
  *Correction:* Every point sweeps exactly the same angle; points farther from the center travel a longer arc length (arc = radius × angle) but the angular displacement is identical, and every point's distance to the center is unchanged. This is precisely why the figure remains rigid and congruent to itself.

**Common mistakes in practice**

- Applying origin rotation rules when the stated center is not the origin
- Mixing up (−y, x) and (y, −x), i.e., rotating 90° in the wrong direction
- Treating a 180° rotation as a reflection (forgetting rotation preserves orientation)
- Assuming positive angles are clockwise
- Forgetting to translate back after rotating in the shifted frame (stopping at step two of the conjugation)
- Sign errors in the general rule, e.g., writing (x cos θ + y sin θ, ...) for the first coordinate instead of (x cos θ − y sin θ, ...)

**Visual teaching opportunities**

- Pin-and-turn transparency: pin a traced figure at the center point and physically rotate it over the original, letting students see the fixed pin, the swept circles, and the absence of any mirror flip.
- Basis-vector tracker: animate where (1, 0) and (0, 1) land under a slowly increasing rotation angle, with the rotation matrix columns updating live — deriving [[cos θ, −sin θ],[sin θ, cos θ]] instead of asserting it.
- Concentric-circles overlay: display circles about the center through each vertex during rotation, making 'each point stays on its own circle and sweeps the same angle' visible.
- Rotation vs. reflection duel: side-by-side animation of a 180° rotation and an x-axis reflection acting on the same labeled triangle, with an orientation arrow flipping only on the reflection side.
- Conjugation storyboard: a three-panel visual for off-origin rotation — panel 1 translate the center to the origin, panel 2 rotate with the clean rule, panel 3 translate back — reinforcing the move-solve-return strategy.

**Worked example**

*Setup:* Rotate the point P(3, 4) by 90° counterclockwise about the origin; verify the result two ways; then rotate Q(2, 1) by 90° counterclockwise about the center C(1, 1) using the translate–rotate–translate strategy.

1. Step 1: Derive the 90° CCW rule by tracking basis directions: (1, 0) must rotate to (0, 1), and (0, 1) must rotate to (−1, 0); so (x, y) = x·(1,0) + y·(0,1) lands at x·(0,1) + y·(−1,0) = (−y, x). Why: Deriving the rule from where two reference directions go means we never have to trust memory — and this derivation is exactly how the rotation matrix's columns are built.
2. Step 2: Apply the rule to P: (3, 4) → (−4, 3). Why: With the rule established, rotation of any point is a single substitution — the payoff of the functional viewpoint.
3. Step 3: Verify distance to the center is preserved: |OP| = √(3² + 4²) = √25 = 5 and |OP′| = √((−4)² + 3²) = √(16 + 9) = √25 = 5. Why: Rotation must keep every point on its circle about the center; equal radii is the fastest arithmetic check that our image is plausible.
4. Step 4: Verify perpendicularity of OP and OP′: the slopes are 4/3 and 3/(−4) = −3/4, and (4/3)·(−3/4) = −1. Why: A 90° rotation must turn the radius OP through a right angle, so OP ⊥ OP′; slope product −1 confirms the angle of rotation is exactly 90°, catching wrong-direction errors that the radius check alone would miss.
5. Step 5: Rotate Q(2, 1) about C(1, 1): first translate so C goes to the origin: Q − C = (2 − 1, 1 − 1) = (1, 0). Why: The clean rule only works at the origin, so we move the whole problem — the conjugation strategy — by subtracting the center from the point.
6. Step 6: Apply the origin rule to the translated point: (1, 0) → (−0, 1) = (0, 1). Why: In translated coordinates the rotation is the easy one we derived in Step 1; this is the 'solve where it's easy' phase of the strategy.
7. Step 7: Translate back by adding C: (0, 1) + (1, 1) = (1, 2), so Q′ = (1, 2). Sanity check: |CQ| = |(1, 0)| = 1 and |CQ′| = |(1, 2) − (1, 1)| = |(0, 1)| = 1, equal radii about C. Why: Undoing the setup translation returns us to the original frame, and the radius check about C (not the origin) confirms the rotation was genuinely centered at C — note the raw origin rule would have given the wrong answer (−1, 2).

*Outcome:* P(3, 4) rotates to P′(−4, 3), verified by equal radii (both 5) and perpendicular radii (slope product (4/3)(−3/4) = −1); Q(2, 1) rotates about C(1, 1) to Q′(1, 2) via translate–rotate–translate, verified by equal distance 1 from C, with the naive origin-rule answer (−1, 2) explicitly recognized as wrong.

**Real-world intuition**

- Game development and computer graphics: every spinning sprite, turning camera, and rotating 3D model is the rotation matrix at work — engines multiply [[cos θ, −sin θ],[sin θ, cos θ]] (and its 3D big siblings) into vertex coordinates every frame, and rotating about an object's own pivot rather than the world origin is exactly the translate–rotate–translate conjugation students learn here.
- Robotics: each joint of a robot arm rotates the links beyond it about the joint's axis; forward kinematics chains these rotations (as matrices) to find the gripper's position, and the counterclockwise-positive angle convention comes straight from this mathematics.
- Aerospace and vehicle attitude control: an aircraft's or spacecraft's orientation is tracked as a composition of rotations (roll, pitch, yaw); flight computers compose rotation matrices to convert between body and world frames, relying on rotations preserving lengths so the aircraft's geometry is never distorted by the math.
- Medical imaging: aligning a patient's scan taken at a slightly rotated angle with a reference scan requires computing the rotation (center and angle) that registers the two images — the 'find the rotation carrying figure A to figure B' problem at clinical stakes.
- Mechanical engineering of gears and turbines: gear teeth are copies of one tooth repeated by rotational symmetry about the axle; a gear with n teeth is invariant under rotation by 360°/n, the design principle that makes gears mesh smoothly.

**Practice progression**

Item types: Apply 90°/180°/270° origin rules to points and triangles, with direction (CW vs. CCW) specified, Determine the rotation (center and angle) that carries a given pre-image onto a given image, Rotate a point about a non-origin center using translate–rotate–translate, Verify a claimed rotation by checking equal radii to the center and the swept angle, Use the general (x cos θ − y sin θ, x sin θ + y cos θ) rule for θ = 30°, 45°, 60° with exact trig values, Distinguish 180° rotation images from reflection images of the same figure using orientation. Suggested item count: 12.

Begin with 90°/180°/270° rotations of single points about the origin, both directions (developing); move to full figures, identifying unknown rotations, and orientation-based rotation-vs-reflection discrimination (developing-to-proficient); progress to off-origin centers via conjugation and verification by radii/angle checks (proficient); cap with general-angle rotations using exact trigonometric values and the matrix form (proficient stretch).

**Assessment objectives**

Formats: Coordinate computation: rotate a figure about the origin and about a non-origin center, showing the translate–rotate–translate steps, Justification item: verify a rotation using equal radii and perpendicular (or correctly angled) radii, Discrimination task: given two images of a labeled triangle, identify which came from a 180° rotation and which from a reflection, citing orientation, Short derivation: obtain the 90° rule by tracking where (1, 0) and (0, 1) land. Bloom alignment: Apply — students execute rotation rules, including conjugation for off-origin centers, on novel figures; derivation and discrimination items reach analysis by requiring justification from invariants (radii, swept angle, orientation)..

Mastery signal: The student can rotate any point by 90°/180°/270° about any given center — automatically invoking translate–rotate–translate when the center is not the origin — can verify the result via distances to the center, and can articulate why a 180° rotation is not a reflection using orientation.

**Teacher notes**

Derive the 90° rule from the basis directions (1,0) → (0,1) and (0,1) → (−1,0) rather than issuing it — students who can re-derive never suffer the (−y, x) vs. (y, −x) confusion, and the derivation is the rotation matrix in embryo. Give the 180°-rotation-versus-reflection contrast a full worked comparison with a labeled triangle, since it is the most persistent misconception at this level. Teach translate–rotate–translate as a named, reusable strategy ('move the problem to where it's easy') so it transfers to off-origin dilations and later to change-of-basis.

**Student notes**

To rotate 90° counterclockwise about the origin, use (x, y) → (−y, x) — and if you forget it, re-derive it by asking where (1, 0) and (0, 1) must land. If the center isn't the origin: subtract the center, rotate with the clean rule, add the center back — always in that order.

**Prerequisite graph**

- Requires: math.geom.transformations, math.trig.trig-functions
- Unlocks (future prerequisite links): none yet mapped
- Cross-topic connections (graph cross-links): none
- Narrative: The rotation matrix is the flagship example of a linear map, connecting directly to math.linalg.rotation-matrix and linear transformations, and its entries are unit-circle trigonometry in action. Later, multiplying complex numbers by cos θ + i sin θ performs the same rotation, and rotational symmetry groups reappear in abstract algebra.

**Teaching hints — review triggers**

- Student cannot state sin θ and cos θ for θ = 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°, 180°, 270° — review math.trig.trig-functions before the general rotation rule.
- Student confuses clockwise and counterclockwise or the sign convention for angles — review directed angles on the unit circle.
- Student cannot compute the distance from a point to the center — review the distance formula from the coordinate plane before radius-preservation checks.
- Student is shaky on the transformation-as-function framing or on fixed-point reasoning — revisit math.geom.transformations before classifying rotations among isometries.

**Spaced repetition / revision guidance**

Re-derive all three special rules (90°, 180°, 270° CCW) from the fate of (1, 0) and (0, 1), then redo one off-origin rotation with the translate–rotate–translate strategy and verify equal radii to the center. Finish by writing one sentence on how a 180° rotation differs from a reflection.

---

### Reflection

*Concept ID: `math.geom.reflection` · Difficulty: proficient · Bloom level: apply · Mastery threshold: 0.85 · Estimated study time: 3h*

**Learning objective.** Students will be able to reflect points and figures over a given line — including the axes, y = x, y = −x, and horizontal/vertical lines — using coordinate rules and the perpendicular-bisector definition, verify that reflections preserve distance while reversing orientation, and explain why reflection is fundamentally different from any rotation of the plane.

A transformation that flips every point over a line of reflection, reversing orientation.

A reflection flips the plane over a line m, called the line of reflection or mirror line. The first-principles definition is geometric and exact: the image P′ of a point P is the point such that m is the perpendicular bisector of segment PP′ — the mirror line sits exactly halfway between every point and its image, meeting the connecting segment at a right angle. Points on m itself don't move at all. Run the two classifying questions: What is fixed? An entire line — the mirror — which no other isometry type fixes (rotations fix one point, translations fix none). What is preserved? All distances and all angle sizes, so reflections are isometries and images are congruent to pre-images. But one thing is reversed: orientation. A triangle labeled counterclockwise reflects to a triangle labeled clockwise, just as a mirror swaps your left and right hands. This orientation reversal is not a defect; it is the defining character of reflection.

On the coordinate plane, the standard mirrors give clean rules, each derivable from the perpendicular-bisector definition: over the x-axis, (x, y) → (x, −y); over the y-axis, (x, y) → (−x, y); over the line y = x, (x, y) → (y, x); over y = −x, (x, y) → (−y, −x); over a vertical line x = a, (x, y) → (2a − x, y), since the image's x-coordinate must sit as far beyond a as x sits before it. The persistent temptation is to believe a reflection is 'really' a rotation by 180° — after all, both seem to flip a figure to the other side. The refutation is concrete: a 180° rotation about the origin sends (x, y) → (−x, −y) and keeps a counterclockwise triangle counterclockwise, while an x-axis reflection sends (x, y) → (x, −y) and makes it clockwise. No rotation, by any angle about any center, can produce a mirror image: to physically reflect a paper triangle you must lift it out of the plane and flip it over — a move through the third dimension that no in-plane turning can imitate.

Reflections are, in a precise sense, the atoms of all isometries: composing two reflections in parallel lines produces a translation (by twice the distance between the lines), composing two reflections in intersecting lines produces a rotation (by twice the angle between them, about their intersection), and a classical theorem says every isometry of the plane is a composition of at most three reflections. Reflection also underlies the concept of line symmetry — a figure has a line of symmetry exactly when it is its own reflection image. Looking forward along the transformations family's bridge to math.linalg.linear-map, reflections over lines through the origin are linear maps with their own 2×2 matrices, and together with rotations they generate the full symmetry structure of the plane; the orientation-reversal students observe here reappears there as a determinant of −1.

**Key ideas**

- Defining property: the mirror line m is the perpendicular bisector of every segment PP′ joining a point to its image — halfway between, at a right angle.
- The fixed-point set is the entire mirror line — the fingerprint separating reflection from rotation (one fixed point) and translation (none).
- Reflections are isometries: distances and angle sizes are preserved and images are congruent — but orientation is reversed, turning counterclockwise labelings clockwise.
- Reflection is not a rotation: (x, y) → (x, −y) over the x-axis versus (x, y) → (−x, −y) for a 180° rotation; no in-plane rotation can create a mirror image.
- Standard coordinate rules, each derivable from the perpendicular-bisector definition: x-axis (x, −y); y-axis (−x, y); y = x gives (y, x); y = −x gives (−y, −x); x = a gives (2a − x, y).
- Reflecting twice over the same line returns every point home: a reflection is its own inverse (an involution).
- Reflections generate everything: two reflections in parallel lines make a translation, two in intersecting lines make a rotation, and every plane isometry is a composition of at most three reflections.

**Vocabulary**

- **line of reflection (mirror line)** — the fixed line over which the plane is flipped; the perpendicular bisector of every point–image segment
- **mirror image** — the reflected figure; congruent to the original but with reversed orientation
- **perpendicular bisector** — a line meeting a segment at its midpoint at 90°; the defining relationship between mirror and point–image segments
- **orientation-reversing** — turning counterclockwise labelings clockwise; the property separating reflections from rotations and translations
- **line symmetry** — a figure's property of being its own reflection image over some line
- **involution** — a transformation that is its own inverse; reflecting twice over the same line is the identity

**Common misconceptions**

- *Misconception:* A reflection is the same as a rotation by 180° — both just flip the shape to the other side.
  *Correction:* They differ in both formula and invariants: reflection over the x-axis is (x, y) → (x, −y), while 180° rotation about the origin is (x, y) → (−x, −y). Decisively, rotation preserves orientation (a counterclockwise triangle stays counterclockwise) while reflection reverses it. No rotation of the plane produces a mirror image — you'd have to flip the plane through 3D space.
- *Misconception:* The image always lands on the 'other side of the graph' (e.g., reflecting always negates both coordinates, or always moves the figure across the origin).
  *Correction:* The image lands on the other side of the mirror line, at equal perpendicular distance — and which coordinates change depends entirely on which line is the mirror. Over y = x nothing is negated at all: (x, y) → (y, x). Points on the mirror don't move. Always identify the mirror line before touching any coordinates.
- *Misconception:* Under reflection, points farther from the mirror line somehow behave differently, or the image's distance from the mirror can differ from the pre-image's.
  *Correction:* Every point's image lies at exactly the same perpendicular distance from the mirror as the original, on the opposite side — that is the definition. A point 5 units in front of the mirror maps to 5 units behind it, along the perpendicular. Unequal distances mean the reflection was performed incorrectly.
- *Misconception:* Reflecting over the line y = x means negating coordinates, i.e., (x, y) → (−x, −y) or (−y, −x).
  *Correction:* Reflection over y = x swaps the coordinates with no sign change: (x, y) → (y, x) — check that (3, 1) and (1, 3) are symmetric across y = x and both distance √2 from it along the perpendicular. The rule (−y, −x) belongs to the different mirror y = −x, and (−x, −y) is a 180° rotation, not a reflection at all.

**Common mistakes in practice**

- Negating the wrong coordinate (using (−x, y) for an x-axis reflection instead of (x, −y))
- Treating reflection over y = x as negation instead of swapping: writing (−x, −y) or (−y, −x) instead of (y, x)
- Confusing a reflection with a 180° rotation and producing (−x, −y) images
- Placing the image on the correct side of the mirror but at the wrong distance (mirror must be the exact midpoint)
- Moving points that lie on the mirror line instead of leaving them fixed
- For mirrors like x = 3, reflecting over the y-axis instead (forgetting the rule (x, y) → (6 − x, y) comes from equal distances to x = 3)

**Visual teaching opportunities**

- Physical mirror or Mira on the mirror line: stand a small mirror along the drawn line m so students see the image exactly where mathematics places it, then verify by folding the paper along m and observing pre-image and image coincide.
- Perpendicular-bisector construction: for one point at a time, draw the perpendicular from P to the mirror, measure the distance, and continue an equal distance beyond — making the definition operational before any coordinate shortcut.
- Orientation flip animation: a labeled triangle with a circulating A→B→C arrow reflects across a line; the arrow visibly reverses from counterclockwise to clockwise, side-by-side with a 180° rotation whose arrow does not reverse.
- Fold-the-plane demo: print a figure, fold the page along the mirror line, and hold it to the light — the crease is the fixed line and the show-through image is the reflection, connecting 'fixed line' to something tangible.
- Two-mirror composition lab: reflect a figure over two parallel lines and observe a translation; reflect over two intersecting lines and observe a rotation — building toward 'reflections generate all isometries'.

**Worked example**

*Setup:* Reflect triangle ABC with vertices A(2, 1), B(5, 1), C(2, 4) over the x-axis. Verify the reflection using the perpendicular-bisector definition, confirm distances are preserved, and show orientation reverses.

1. Step 1: Identify the mirror line and its coordinate rule: the mirror is the x-axis (the line y = 0), and reflecting over it sends (x, y) → (x, −y). Why: Every reflection problem starts by fixing the mirror; the rule 'negate y' is just the perpendicular-bisector definition specialized to a horizontal mirror — the perpendicular direction is vertical, so only y changes.
2. Step 2: Apply the rule to each vertex: A(2, 1) → A′(2, −1); B(5, 1) → B′(5, −1); C(2, 4) → C′(2, −4). Why: The triangle's image is determined by its vertex images, and the identical rule applies to each point because reflection is a function on the whole plane.
3. Step 3: Verify the perpendicular-bisector definition on C: the segment from C(2, 4) to C′(2, −4) is vertical (perpendicular to the x-axis), and its midpoint is (2, (4 + (−4))/2) = (2, 0), which lies on the mirror. Why: This checks the actual definition — mirror as perpendicular bisector of PP′ — rather than trusting the shortcut rule; C sits 4 above the axis and C′ sits 4 below, equal distances on opposite sides.
4. Step 4: Verify distances are preserved: AB = |5 − 2| = 3 and A′B′ = |5 − 2| = 3; AC = |4 − 1| = 3 and A′C′ = |−4 − (−1)| = |−3| = 3; BC = √((5−2)² + (1−4)²) = √(9 + 9) = √18 = 3√2 and B′C′ = √((5−2)² + (−1−(−4))²) = √(9 + 9) = 3√2. Why: Confirming all three sides — including the slanted hypotenuse — establishes the image is congruent to the original, the isometry property, and guards against errors that axis-aligned checks alone would miss.
5. Step 5: Check orientation: traversing A(2,1) → B(5,1) → C(2,4) goes counterclockwise (right along the base, then up-left), while traversing A′(2,−1) → B′(5,−1) → C′(2,−4) goes clockwise (right along the base, then down-left). Why: Orientation reversal is the signature of reflection — this is the exact check that would expose a student who performed a 180° rotation (which would give (−2, −1), (−5, −1), (−2, −4) and keep the traversal counterclockwise) instead of a reflection.
6. Step 6: Identify the fixed points and confirm the classification: a point is fixed exactly when (x, y) = (x, −y), i.e., y = 0 — the entire x-axis, and nothing else. Why: A whole line of fixed points is the fingerprint of reflection among the isometries; combined with preserved distances and reversed orientation, this completes a full classification-by-invariants of what we did.
7. Step 7: Double-check with the involution property: reflecting A′(2, −1) over the x-axis returns (2, 1) = A. Why: A reflection must undo itself; this one-step self-inverse check is a fast, always-available error detector for any reflection computation.

*Outcome:* Image triangle A′(2, −1), B′(5, −1), C′(2, −4); perpendicular-bisector definition verified on CC′ (vertical segment, midpoint (2, 0) on the mirror); all three side lengths preserved (3, 3, 3√2); orientation confirmed reversed from counterclockwise to clockwise; fixed-point set identified as exactly the x-axis; and the self-inverse check returning A′ to A.

**Real-world intuition**

- Optics and everyday mirrors: the law of reflection makes a plane mirror produce exactly the geometric reflection of the scene — your mirror image's reversed handedness (left–right swap) is the orientation reversal of this concept, and periscopes and kaleidoscopes chain multiple reflections just like two-mirror composition exercises.
- Computer graphics and game development: environment reflections in water or on shiny floors are rendered by reflecting the camera or scene geometry across the reflective plane and redrawing — a reflection matrix applied to every vertex; sprite mirroring (a character turning to face left) is a one-axis reflection, cheaper than storing duplicate art.
- Engineering and design symmetry: aircraft, cars, bridges, and buildings are designed with bilateral (mirror) symmetry so that mass and aerodynamic forces balance; CAD software's 'mirror' command constructs the reflection image of half a part, guaranteeing perfect symmetry by mathematics rather than by hand.
- Molecular chemistry and pharmacology: chiral molecules exist in left- and right-handed mirror forms (enantiomers) that no rotation can superimpose — the chemical embodiment of 'reflection is not a rotation' — and the two forms can have drastically different biological effects, so drug manufacturing must control which mirror form is produced.
- Signal processing and typography: even/odd symmetry of functions (f(−x) = ±f(x)) is reflection symmetry of graphs across the y-axis, exploited to halve computation in Fourier analysis; typeface designers use reflection to audit letterforms like A, H, T that carry mirror symmetry.

**Practice progression**

Item types: Reflect points and polygons over the x-axis, y-axis, y = x, and y = −x using coordinate rules, Reflect over vertical/horizontal lines x = a and y = b using the equal-distances-from-the-mirror reasoning, Identify the mirror line given a pre-image/image pair (construct the perpendicular bisector of PP′), Verify a reflection via midpoint-on-mirror and perpendicularity checks, Discriminate reflection images from 180°-rotation images of the same labeled figure using orientation, Find lines of symmetry of figures and letters (a figure is symmetric when it equals its own reflection image). Suggested item count: 12.

Start with axis reflections of single points and triangles (developing); add y = x and y = −x rules and the swap-vs-negate distinctions (developing-to-proficient); progress to non-axis mirrors x = a and y = b, recovering unknown mirror lines from point-image pairs, and orientation-based reflection-vs-rotation discrimination (proficient); finish with two-mirror composition observations (parallel → translation, intersecting → rotation) as a stretch (proficient).

**Assessment objectives**

Formats: Coordinate computation: reflect a figure over a specified line and verify one vertex via the perpendicular-bisector definition, Reverse problem: determine the mirror line from a given pre-image/image pair, Discrimination item: identify which of two images is the reflection and which the 180° rotation, citing orientation, Short justification: explain why points on the mirror line do not move. Bloom alignment: Apply — students execute reflection rules over varied mirror lines and apply the perpendicular-bisector definition to verify and to recover mirrors; discrimination items reach analysis via classification by invariants (fixed line, reversed orientation)..

Mastery signal: The student can reflect any figure over any of the standard mirror lines without conflating the rules, can recover an unknown mirror as the perpendicular bisector of a point-image segment, and can reliably tell a reflection from a 180° rotation by checking orientation — articulating that mirror images cannot be produced by any in-plane rotation.

**Teacher notes**

Teach the perpendicular-bisector definition operationally (drop the perpendicular, copy the distance to the other side) before any coordinate shortcut, so the rules for unusual mirrors like x = 3 can be reconstructed rather than memorized. Devote a full contrast activity to reflection versus 180° rotation with a labeled triangle — it is the most stubborn misconception in this cluster and only an orientation check dislodges it. The fold test (fold along the mirror; pre-image and image must coincide) is the fastest whole-class verification tool and doubles as the definition of line symmetry.

**Student notes**

To reflect a point, travel from it perpendicular to the mirror line, then continue the exact same distance on the other side — the mirror is always the halfway line. Quick rules: x-axis negates y; y-axis negates x; y = x swaps the coordinates; and reflecting twice always brings you home.

**Prerequisite graph**

- Requires: math.geom.transformations
- Unlocks (future prerequisite links): none yet mapped
- Cross-topic connections (graph cross-links): none
- Narrative: Reflections over lines through the origin are linear maps whose matrices have determinant −1, feeding directly into math.linalg.linear-map and the transformations family's bridge to linear algebra. Function symmetry (even functions as y-axis-symmetric graphs), the optics law of reflection, and chirality in chemistry all reuse this concept, and the two-mirror composition results preview how reflections generate all plane isometries.

**Teaching hints — review triggers**

- Student cannot compute midpoints or distances between coordinate points — review the midpoint and distance formulas from the coordinate plane before perpendicular-bisector verification.
- Student is unsure what perpendicular means or how perpendicularity shows up in slopes — review perpendicular lines before non-axis mirrors.
- Student cannot state the transformation-as-function framing or the meaning of isometry and orientation — revisit math.geom.transformations before classifying reflections.
- Student confuses the graphs of y = x, y = −x, x = a, and y = b — review line equations before applying mirror-specific rules.

**Spaced repetition / revision guidance**

Rewrite the four standard rules (x-axis, y-axis, y = x, y = −x) from memory, then verify one of them from scratch using the perpendicular-bisector definition on a specific point. Finish with the discrimination drill: given (x, y) → (x, −y) and (x, y) → (−x, −y), state which is the reflection, which is the rotation, and which invariant tells them apart.

---

### Dilation

*Concept ID: `math.geom.dilation` · Difficulty: proficient · Bloom level: apply · Mastery threshold: 0.8 · Estimated study time: 3h*

**Learning objective.** Students will be able to perform dilations with a given center and scale factor k, use the origin-centered rule (x, y) → (kx, ky) and its off-center generalization, explain why dilation preserves angles and shape while scaling all lengths by |k| (and areas by k²), and connect dilation to similarity of figures.

A transformation scaling distances from a center by a constant factor k; produces a similar figure of different size.

A dilation is the transformation that scales the plane from a fixed center C by a constant factor k: every point P maps to the point P′ on ray CP (for k > 0) with CP′ = k · CP. With center at the origin, the coordinate rule is beautifully simple: (x, y) → (kx, ky). Run the classifying questions from the transformations framework. What is fixed? Exactly the center — the one point whose distance from itself is zero no matter the scaling. What is preserved? Not distance: dilation is deliberately not an isometry when |k| ≠ 1, since every length gets multiplied by |k|. But everything about shape survives: every angle keeps its exact measure, every line maps to a line (lines through the center map to themselves; all other lines map to parallel lines), and ratios between lengths are unchanged — if one side of a figure is twice another, the same is true in the image. This precise combination — angles preserved, all lengths scaled uniformly — is what 'same shape, different size' means, and it is the definition of similarity: two figures are similar exactly when a dilation composed with an isometry carries one onto the other.

The scale factor tells the whole story: k > 1 enlarges, 0 < k < 1 shrinks toward the center, k = 1 is the identity, and negative k sends points to the opposite side of the center while scaling by |k| (a dilation with k = −1 is the same as a 180° rotation about the center). One subtlety with real consequences: lengths scale by |k| but areas scale by k², because area is length times length — a triangle dilated by k = 2 has sides twice as long but area four times as large. Confusing these two scaling rates is a classic and costly error, from geometry exams to construction estimates. Note also what dilation does not do: it never distorts, shears, or stretches one direction more than another — that would break angle preservation. A figure and its dilation image are never 'a different shape'; they are the same shape at a different size, which is why photographs enlarge without warping.

Dilation is the engine behind the entire theory of similar figures — the parallel side-splitting of math.geom.similar-triangles is dilation in action, since a dilation centered at a triangle's vertex produces exactly the nested similar triangle configuration. Off-center dilations reduce to the origin case by the same translate–scale–translate conjugation strategy used for rotations. And looking down the transformations family's forward bridge to math.linalg.linear-map: the origin-centered dilation (x, y) → (kx, ky) is the simplest interesting linear map of all — scalar multiplication, with matrix kI — and together with rotations it generates the similarity transformations of the plane. Dilation is also your first encounter with the scaling reasoning that later governs derivatives (local linear scaling) and map projections.

**Key ideas**

- Dilation with center C and factor k sends P to the point P′ on ray CP with CP′ = k · CP; with center at the origin the rule is (x, y) → (kx, ky).
- The center is the only fixed point; distances from the center — and in fact all distances — scale by |k|, so dilation is not an isometry unless |k| = 1.
- Angles are exactly preserved and length ratios within the figure are unchanged, so the image is similar to the original: same shape, different size — never distorted.
- The scale factor's regimes: k > 1 enlarges, 0 < k < 1 shrinks toward the center, k = 1 is the identity, k < 0 flips through the center while scaling by |k| (k = −1 equals a 180° rotation).
- Lengths scale by |k| but areas scale by k² — a dilation by 3 makes sides 3 times longer and areas 9 times larger.
- Lines through the center map to themselves; every other line maps to a parallel line — the geometric source of the parallel configurations in similar-triangle theorems.
- Dilations compose by multiplying factors (k₁ then k₂ gives k₁k₂ about the same center) and invert by the reciprocal: the inverse of scaling by k is scaling by 1/k.

**Vocabulary**

- **dilation** — the transformation scaling every point's distance from a fixed center by the factor k; rule (x, y) → (kx, ky) when centered at the origin
- **center of dilation** — the unique fixed point from which all distances are scaled
- **scale factor k** — the multiplier applied to every length; k > 1 enlarges, 0 < |k| < 1 reduces, negative k flips through the center
- **enlargement / reduction** — dilations with |k| > 1 / |k| < 1
- **similar figures** — figures with equal angles and proportional sides; exactly the figures related by a dilation composed with an isometry
- **square-cube scaling** — the principle that lengths scale by k, areas by k², volumes by k³

**Common misconceptions**

- *Misconception:* Dilation changes the shape of a figure — a dilated triangle is a 'different triangle' with different angles.
  *Correction:* Dilation changes size only, never shape: every angle keeps its exact measure and all side-length ratios are preserved, so the image is similar to the original. A 30-60-90 triangle dilated by any factor is still a 30-60-90 triangle. If angles changed, the transformation performed was not a dilation.
- *Misconception:* Dilation is an isometry like the other transformations, so the image is congruent to the pre-image.
  *Correction:* Dilation with |k| ≠ 1 multiplies every distance by |k|, so image and pre-image are similar but not congruent. Congruence (via isometries) and similarity (via dilation plus isometries) are distinct relationships; dilation is precisely the transformation that separates them.
- *Misconception:* When a figure is dilated by k, its area also scales by k.
  *Correction:* Area scales by k², because both length dimensions scale by k. A triangle with area 6 dilated by k = 2 has area 24, not 12. This length-vs-area scaling distinction extends to volume (k³) and is one of the most practically consequential facts in all of geometry.
- *Misconception:* The rule (x, y) → (kx, ky) works for any center of dilation.
  *Correction:* That rule is valid only when the center is the origin, because it scales position vectors measured from the origin. For center C(a, b), conjugate: P′ = C + k(P − C), i.e., subtract the center, scale, add the center back. Dilating (3, 1) by k = 2 about center (1, 1) gives (5, 1), not (6, 2).
- *Misconception:* A negative scale factor is impossible, or it just means 'shrink'.
  *Correction:* Negative k is legitimate and geometrically meaningful: the image lies on the opposite side of the center, at distance |k| · CP. Shrinking is the job of 0 < |k| < 1; the sign controls which side of the center the image lands on. In particular k = −1 flips every point through the center — a 180° rotation.

**Common mistakes in practice**

- Using (kx, ky) when the center is not the origin, instead of P′ = C + k(P − C)
- Scaling area by k instead of k² (and volume by k instead of k³)
- Adding k to coordinates instead of multiplying by it
- Treating a dilated image as congruent to the original, or conversely claiming dilation 'changes the shape'
- Mishandling negative k — ignoring the flip through the center, or treating negative factors as impossible
- Scaling only some vertices, or scaling x-coordinates and y-coordinates by different factors (which distorts and is not a dilation)

**Visual teaching opportunities**

- Ray-projection construction: from the center, draw rays through each vertex and mark images at k times the distance along each ray with a ruler — the 'projector beam' picture that makes the definition physical.
- Slider-driven dilation applet: drag k continuously from 3 down through 1, to 0.5, to −1, watching the figure enlarge, pass through congruence, shrink toward the center, collapse, and re-emerge flipped on the far side.
- Angle-invariance spotlight: overlay angle-measure readouts on a figure and its dilation image, showing every angle identical while side-length labels scale — the same-shape-different-size message in one picture.
- Area-vs-length tiling: dilate a unit square by k = 2 and k = 3 and tile the images with copies of the original, physically counting 4 and 9 unit squares to cement the k² area law.
- Camera/shadow demonstration: a flashlight (center) casting a shadow of a cutout onto a wall enacts dilation from a point — moving the cutout changes the scale factor, and the shadow never distorts the shape.

**Worked example**

*Setup:* Dilate triangle ABC with vertices A(2, 2), B(6, 2), C(2, 5) from the origin with scale factor k = 2. Verify the length and area scaling laws, confirm angles are preserved, and contrast with the off-center version about C₀(2, 2).

1. Step 1: Apply the origin-centered rule (x, y) → (2x, 2y) to each vertex: A(2, 2) → A′(4, 4); B(6, 2) → B′(12, 4); C(2, 5) → C′(4, 10). Why: With the center at the origin, dilation is coordinate scaling — each position vector from the center is stretched to twice its length, which is exactly the definition CP′ = k · CP in coordinates.
2. Step 2: Verify the side lengths scale by k = 2: AB = |6 − 2| = 4 and A′B′ = |12 − 4| = 8 = 2 × 4; AC = |5 − 2| = 3 and A′C′ = |10 − 4| = 6 = 2 × 3. Why: The defining promise of dilation is that every length is multiplied by |k|; checking both legs confirms the scaling is uniform in all directions, not a one-direction stretch.
3. Step 3: Verify the hypotenuse too: BC = √((6−2)² + (2−5)²) = √(16 + 9) = √25 = 5, and B′C′ = √((12−4)² + (4−10)²) = √(64 + 36) = √100 = 10 = 2 × 5. Why: A slanted side could expose a non-uniform scaling that axis-aligned sides hide; 5 → 10 confirms genuine uniform scaling — and the 3-4-5 triangle becoming 6-8-10 shows side ratios (3:4:5) are perfectly preserved.
4. Step 4: Confirm shape preservation via ratios and angles: original ratios 3:4:5 and image ratios 6:8:10 = 3:4:5 are identical, and since 3² + 4² = 5² and 6² + 8² = 10², both triangles are right triangles with the right angle at A and A′ respectively. Why: Equal ratios plus equal angles is precisely what 'similar' means — the image is the same shape at double size, which is dilation's essential character.
5. Step 5: Verify the area law: original area = ½ × 4 × 3 = 6; image area = ½ × 8 × 6 = 24, and 24 = 2² × 6 = 4 × 6. Why: Lengths scale by k but area scales by k² because two length dimensions each contribute a factor of k — the single most-tested and most-confused consequence of dilation.
6. Step 6: Check the fixed point and collinearity with the center: the origin maps to itself, and each image vertex lies on the ray from the origin through its pre-image — e.g., A(2, 2) and A′(4, 4) both lie on the ray y = x, with |OA′| = √32 = 2√2 · 2... recomputing: |OA| = √(4+4) = 2√2 and |OA′| = √(16+16) = 4√2 = 2 × 2√2. Why: Center fixed and images along rays through the center at k times the distance is the full geometric definition; verifying it ties the coordinate shortcut back to first principles.
7. Step 7: Contrast with center C₀(2, 2) = A: using P′ = C₀ + 2(P − C₀), vertex A maps to (2,2) + 2(0,0) = (2, 2) — A is now fixed; B maps to (2,2) + 2(4,0) = (10, 2); C maps to (2,2) + 2(0,3) = (2, 8). Why: The same scale factor about a different center fixes a different point and lands the image elsewhere (though still a congruent copy of A′B′C′'s shape) — demonstrating why the (kx, ky) shortcut must never be used when the center is not the origin.

*Outcome:* Origin-centered image A′(4, 4), B′(12, 4), C′(4, 10) with all sides doubled (4→8, 3→6, 5→10), ratios 3:4:5 preserved, right angle preserved, area quadrupled (6 → 24 = 2² × 6), origin fixed, and each image vertex on the ray through its pre-image at twice the distance; the off-center version about (2, 2) yielding A(2,2) fixed, B′(10, 2), C′(2, 8), proving the center changes the outcome.

**Real-world intuition**

- Computer graphics and responsive design: pinch-to-zoom on a phone, scaling a game sprite, and resizing vector graphics are dilations applied via scaling matrices; because dilation preserves angles and ratios, zoomed content stays undistorted, and graphics pipelines compose scale matrices with rotations and translations for every rendered frame.
- Maps, blueprints, and scale models: a 1:100 architectural plan is a dilation with k = 1/100 — every length on paper is 1/100 of reality while every angle is exact, which is why builders can measure angles directly off a plan but must multiply lengths by the scale factor; model engineering uses the k² and k³ laws to reason about area and weight of scaled prototypes.
- Photography, projection, and vision: a camera or projector performs a dilation from its optical center — the image on a movie screen is a large-k dilation of the film frame, undistorted precisely because dilation preserves shape; the pinhole-camera geometry of similar triangles is this dilation drawn as rays.
- Biology and engineering scaling laws: the k² area versus k³ volume mismatch (the square-cube law) explains why an elephant needs proportionally thicker legs than a gazelle and why small drones are structurally easier than flying cars — strength grows with cross-sectional area (k²) but weight grows with volume (k³).
- Medical and scientific imaging: zooming microscope and radiology images are dilations, and measurements taken on-screen must be divided by the display scale factor; calibration bars on micrographs exist exactly so lengths can be converted through the known k.

**Practice progression**

Item types: Apply origin-centered dilations (k > 1, 0 < k < 1, and k < 0) to points and polygons, Perform off-center dilations using P′ = C + k(P − C), Determine the scale factor and center from a pre-image/image pair (rays through corresponding points meet at the center), Compute image perimeters and areas from the scaling laws (|k| for lengths, k² for areas) without re-measuring, Decide whether two given figures are related by a dilation (check ratios and collinearity with a candidate center), Mixed classification: sort transformations into congruence-producing (isometries) vs. similarity-producing (dilations included), with justification. Suggested item count: 12.

Begin with origin-centered enlargements of single points and triangles (developing); add fractional and negative scale factors and perimeter/area scaling computations (developing-to-proficient); progress to off-center dilations via conjugation and to recovering the center and factor from figure pairs (proficient); finish with similarity arguments — proving two figures are or are not dilation images of each other (proficient).

**Assessment objectives**

Formats: Coordinate computation: dilate a figure about the origin and about a non-origin center, showing the subtract–scale–add steps, Quantitative reasoning: given a scale factor, predict image perimeter and area and justify the k vs. k² distinction, Reverse-engineering task: find the center and scale factor from a diagram of a figure and its image, Concept check: explain why a dilated figure is similar but not congruent to the original. Bloom alignment: Apply — students execute dilation rules for varied centers and factors and apply the scaling laws to compute derived quantities; center-recovery and similarity-justification items reach analysis through reasoning about ratios, collinearity, and invariants..

Mastery signal: The student can dilate any figure about any center (automatically conjugating for off-origin centers), predicts perimeter scaling by |k| and area scaling by k² without hesitation, handles negative and fractional factors correctly, and explains that dilation preserves shape exactly — producing similar, not congruent, figures.

**Teacher notes**

Insist on the ray construction (center, rays through vertices, images at k times the distance) before any coordinate shortcut, and immediately follow the (kx, ky) rule with an off-center counterexample so students never over-apply it. The k-versus-k² distinction deserves its own tiling activity — students who physically count four unit squares inside the doubled square retain the area law far better than those told it. Frame dilation explicitly as 'the transformation that defines similarity', so the similar-triangles unit reads as applied dilation rather than a fresh subject.

**Student notes**

A dilation is a zoom from a center point: every point slides along its ray from the center to k times its original distance, so shapes keep every angle and just change size. Remember the two scaling speeds — lengths multiply by k, but areas multiply by k² (double the sides, quadruple the area).

**Prerequisite graph**

- Requires: math.geom.transformations, math.arith.ratios
- Unlocks (future prerequisite links): none yet mapped
- Cross-topic connections (graph cross-links): none
- Narrative: Dilation is the transformation engine behind math.geom.similar-triangles — a dilation at a vertex produces the nested parallel-side configuration of the basic proportionality theorem — and origin-centered dilation is scalar multiplication, the simplest linear map on the road to math.linalg.linear-map. The k²/k³ scaling laws reappear in physics and biology as the square-cube law, and in calculus the derivative describes local scaling of exactly this kind.

**Teaching hints — review triggers**

- Student cannot form or interpret ratios and scale factors (e.g., 3:4:5 or 'twice as long') — review math.arith.ratios before scaling arguments.
- Student cannot multiply coordinates by fractions or negatives accurately — remediate rational-number arithmetic before fractional/negative k problems.
- Student is unsure of the transformation framework (fixed points, preserved properties, isometry vs. not) — revisit math.geom.transformations first.
- Student cannot compute simple areas (triangle, rectangle) — review area formulas before the k² scaling law.

**Spaced repetition / revision guidance**

Redo one dilation three ways — origin center, off-origin center via subtract-scale-add, and negative k — and verify a slanted side scales by |k| each time. Then close with the two-line self-quiz: perimeter of a k = 3 dilation image is how many times the original (3), and its area (9)?

---

### Vectors in 2D

*Concept ID: `math.geom.vectors-2d` · Difficulty: proficient · Bloom level: apply · Mastery threshold: 0.8 · Estimated study time: 6h*

**Learning objective.** Students will be able to represent 2D vectors both geometrically (as arrows with magnitude and direction) and in components (a, b), add and subtract vectors head-to-tail and componentwise, multiply vectors by scalars, compute magnitudes via the Pythagorean theorem, and translate fluently between displacement stories and component arithmetic.

A quantity with both magnitude and direction in the plane, represented by an ordered pair; added head-to-tail and scaled by multiplication.

A vector is a quantity carrying two pieces of information at once: how much and which way — magnitude and direction. The first-principles model, deliberately coordinate-free, is displacement: 'walk 5 km northeast' is a vector; 'walk 5 km' is not (no direction), and 'the flagpole at the park' is not (that's a position, not a journey). An arrow drawn anywhere in the plane represents a vector, and — this is the crucial convention — two arrows represent the same vector whenever they have equal length and point the same way, regardless of where they start. A vector is the journey, not the starting point. This is exactly the translation vector from transformation geometry, now promoted to an object of study in its own right: each vector is an instruction that can be applied anywhere.

Coordinates enter as bookkeeping for displacement: the vector (a, b) means 'a units in the x-direction and b units in the y-direction', and the arrow from point P(x₁, y₁) to point Q(x₂, y₂) has components (x₂ − x₁, y₂ − y₁) — always head minus tail. The magnitude is forced by the Pythagorean theorem: |v| = √(a² + b²), since the displacement is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs a and b. Addition has two equivalent faces that must be connected, not chosen between: geometrically, u + v means 'do journey u, then journey v from where you arrived' (head-to-tail), and algebraically the components simply add, (u₁ + v₁, u₂ + v₂), because the total east-west progress is the sum of the east-west parts. Scalar multiplication kv scales the length by |k| and reverses direction if k < 0, and subtraction u − v = u + (−v) answers 'what journey takes me from the head of v to the head of u?'. A subtle but vital fact: magnitudes do not simply add — |u + v| ≤ |u| + |v| (the triangle inequality), with equality only when u and v point the same way, because journeys in different directions partially cancel.

Vectors must be carefully distinguished from the points they superficially resemble: (3, 4) as a point is a location; (3, 4) as a vector is a displacement that can be applied to any location. The connection is that a point's position vector is the displacement from the origin to it — useful, but origin-dependent, whereas the vector itself is not. This concept directly unlocks math.geom.vectors-3d, where the same displacement logic extends to triples (a, b, c) with essentially nothing new to believe, and math.linalg.vector, where linear algebra rebuilds vectors abstractly as objects you can add and scale — the axioms distilled from exactly the arithmetic learned here. Physics runs on this concept: velocity, force, and acceleration are all vectors, and 'resolve the force into components' is precisely the (a, b) decomposition.

**Key ideas**

- A vector encodes magnitude and direction together — the model is displacement ('the journey'), not position ('the place'); arrows with equal length and direction are the same vector wherever drawn.
- Components are displacement bookkeeping: (a, b) means a across and b up; the vector from P to Q is head minus tail, (x₂ − x₁, y₂ − y₁).
- Magnitude comes from the Pythagorean theorem: |(a, b)| = √(a² + b²) — the displacement is the hypotenuse of its component right triangle.
- Addition is journey-composition: head-to-tail geometrically, componentwise algebraically — and the two descriptions are the same fact in two languages.
- Scalar multiplication kv stretches length by |k| and flips direction when k < 0; subtraction u − v is the vector from the head of v to the head of u (when tails coincide).
- The triangle inequality |u + v| ≤ |u| + |v|: magnitudes of journeys in different directions do not add, because progress in one direction partially cancels progress in another.
- Vector arithmetic is commutative and associative (the parallelogram picture shows u + v = v + u), and the zero vector is the do-nothing journey — the algebraic structure linear algebra will later axiomatize.

**Vocabulary**

- **vector** — a quantity with magnitude and direction, modeled as a displacement; written (a, b) in components
- **magnitude |v|** — the length of a vector, computed as √(a² + b²)
- **components** — the horizontal and vertical parts (a, b) of a vector's displacement
- **resultant** — the single vector equal to a sum of vectors; the net journey
- **head-to-tail addition** — placing the tail of the second vector at the head of the first; the resultant runs from first tail to last head
- **scalar** — a plain number (no direction) used to scale a vector; kv has length |k||v| and flips direction if k < 0

**Common misconceptions**

- *Misconception:* A vector is the same thing as a point — (3, 4) just names the spot three across and four up.
  *Correction:* A point is a location; a vector is a displacement between locations. The vector (3, 4) can start anywhere: applied at (0, 0) it lands at (3, 4), applied at (10, 2) it lands at (13, 6). Position vectors (from the origin) link the two ideas, but the vector itself carries no starting point — that is exactly why one arrow drawn at the origin and an identical arrow drawn elsewhere are equal as vectors.
- *Misconception:* The magnitude of a sum is the sum of the magnitudes: |u + v| = |u| + |v|.
  *Correction:* Only when u and v point in exactly the same direction. In general |u + v| ≤ |u| + |v| because journeys in different directions partially cancel: for u = (3, 4) and v = (−1, 2), |u| + |v| = 5 + √5 ≈ 7.24, but u + v = (2, 6) has magnitude √40 = 2√10 ≈ 6.32. Walking 5 km then 2.2 km does not leave you 7.2 km from home unless both legs were the same direction.
- *Misconception:* Two arrows drawn at different places are different vectors, even if they have the same length and direction.
  *Correction:* By definition, vectors are equal exactly when their magnitudes and directions agree — location is not part of the data. An arrow from (0,0) to (2,1) and an arrow from (5,5) to (7,6) are the same vector (2, 1). This convention is what lets us slide arrows head-to-tail when adding, and it is the same idea as a translation acting identically on every point.
- *Misconception:* When computing the vector from P to Q, subtract in either order — or take Q's coordinates as the vector.
  *Correction:* The vector from P to Q is always head minus tail: Q − P = (x₂ − x₁, y₂ − y₁). Reversing the order gives the opposite vector (the journey from Q back to P), and using Q's raw coordinates gives the position vector of Q from the origin — a different journey entirely unless P is the origin.
- *Misconception:* Multiplying a vector by a negative scalar makes its magnitude negative.
  *Correction:* Magnitude is a length and is never negative: |kv| = |k| · |v|. A negative scalar reverses the direction, not the size — (−2)·(3, 4) = (−6, −8) has magnitude √(36+64) = 10 = 2 × 5, pointing opposite to (3, 4).

**Common mistakes in practice**

- Adding magnitudes instead of components: claiming |u + v| = |u| + |v| for non-parallel vectors
- Subtracting tail minus head (or using the endpoint's coordinates) when finding the vector between two points
- Treating vectors as points — assuming a vector 'lives' at a specific location
- Forgetting to square-root in magnitude computations (reporting a² + b² instead of √(a² + b²)) or adding a + b instead
- Sign errors when components are negative, especially in subtraction like 4 − (−2)
- Saying a magnitude is negative after scalar multiplication by a negative number

**Visual teaching opportunities**

- Treasure-map walk: students physically execute 'journey u then journey v' on a floor grid, marking start and end, then verify the single equivalent displacement matches componentwise addition — head-to-tail made kinesthetic.
- Equal-vector field: draw the same vector (2, 1) as arrows anchored at six scattered points, visually enforcing 'same length + same direction = same vector, location irrelevant'.
- Parallelogram vs. triangle addition toggle: an applet showing u + v built both head-to-tail and as the parallelogram diagonal, with u + v and v + u visibly coinciding — commutativity as a picture.
- Component staircase: display any vector as its horizontal leg then vertical leg (the right triangle), with the magnitude computed live via the Pythagorean theorem as the arrow is dragged.
- Cancellation demonstration: animate u = (3, 4) followed by v = (−1, 2), plotting straight-line distance from start against total distance walked, making the triangle inequality's 'partial cancellation' visible and quantitative.

**Worked example**

*Setup:* A hiker starts at base camp, walks with displacement u = (3, 4) km (3 km east, 4 km north), then walks with displacement v = (−1, 2) km. Find the total displacement, the distance from camp, compare it with the total distance walked, and find what single displacement would return the hiker to camp.

1. Step 1: Represent each leg as a vector and compute each magnitude: |u| = √(3² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5 km, and |v| = √((−1)² + 2²) = √(1 + 4) = √5 ≈ 2.24 km. Why: Components encode each journey's east-west and north-south parts, and the Pythagorean theorem converts components to straight-line length because the displacement is the hypotenuse of its component triangle.
2. Step 2: Add the vectors componentwise: u + v = (3 + (−1), 4 + 2) = (2, 6). Why: The hiker's total eastward progress is the sum of the eastward parts (3 − 1 = 2) and total northward progress is the sum of the northward parts (4 + 2 = 6) — componentwise addition is just accounting for each direction separately.
3. Step 3: Draw the head-to-tail picture: place u's tail at camp, then v's tail at u's head; the resultant u + v runs from camp straight to the final position. Why: The geometric and algebraic definitions of addition must be seen as the same fact — 'do one journey, then the other' lands exactly where the component sums say, which is what makes vector algebra trustworthy for real navigation.
4. Step 4: Compute the distance from camp: |u + v| = √(2² + 6²) = √(4 + 36) = √40 = 2√10 ≈ 6.32 km. Why: Distance from camp is the magnitude of the net displacement — a single application of the same Pythagorean conversion — and not something computable from |u| and |v| alone without direction information.
5. Step 5: Compare with the total distance walked: the hiker walked |u| + |v| = 5 + √5 ≈ 7.24 km, but is only ≈ 6.32 km from camp; 6.32 < 7.24 confirms |u + v| < |u| + |v|. Why: The two legs point in different directions, so some progress cancels — this concrete gap between path length and net displacement is the triangle inequality, and conflating the two is the single most common vector error.
6. Step 6: Find the return displacement: the journey home is the opposite of the net displacement, −(u + v) = (−2, −6), with magnitude √((−2)² + (−6)²) = √40 = 2√10 ≈ 6.32 km. Why: Adding a vector and its negative gives the zero vector — camp to summit-point plus summit-point to camp is the do-nothing journey — showing how additive inverses answer 'how do I get back?' questions.
7. Step 7: Sanity-check with scalar structure: the return vector (−2, −6) = (−1)(2, 6) has the same magnitude and exactly reversed direction, and note (2, 6) = 2·(1, 3), so the net displacement is parallel to (1, 3). Why: Scalar multiples encode 'same or opposite direction, scaled length' — recognizing parallel vectors by factoring out scalars is a skill that pays off throughout vector geometry.

*Outcome:* Net displacement u + v = (2, 6) km with |u + v| = √40 = 2√10 ≈ 6.32 km from camp; total path length 5 + √5 ≈ 7.24 km, strictly exceeding the net displacement (triangle inequality verified numerically); return vector (−2, −6) with equal magnitude 2√10; and the parallel-direction observation (2, 6) = 2(1, 3).

**Real-world intuition**

- Game physics and character movement: a game character's velocity is a 2D vector updated every frame — pressing two arrow keys adds two direction vectors, knockback adds an impulse vector, and the engine moves the character by position += velocity × dt; diagonal-movement speed bugs in games are literally triangle-inequality mistakes (adding magnitudes instead of vectors).
- Aviation and marine navigation: a plane's airspeed vector plus the wind vector equals its ground-track vector — pilots compute crab angles by vector addition, and the reason a headwind lengthens a flight is component cancellation along the flight direction.
- Force analysis in physics and engineering: multiple forces on an object combine by vector addition, and equilibrium means the force vectors sum to zero; resolving a force on a ramp into components along and perpendicular to the surface is the (a, b) decomposition in action.
- Robotics and drones: a delivery drone's path is planned as a sequence of displacement vectors; its onboard controller continuously computes the vector from current position to target (head minus tail) to decide its heading, and returns home by negating its net displacement.
- Computer graphics and UI: sprite motion, camera panning, gradient directions, and touch-gesture deltas are all 2D vectors; a drag gesture reports the displacement vector from touch-down to touch-up, which apps scale (scalar multiplication) for momentum scrolling.

**Practice progression**

Item types: Convert between displacement stories ('4 km west, 3 km north') and component vectors, and back, Compute the vector between two points (head minus tail) and its magnitude, Add and subtract vectors componentwise and verify one case with a head-to-tail sketch, Scalar multiplication problems including negative and fractional scalars, with magnitude checks |kv| = |k||v|, Triangle-inequality reasoning: decide when |u + v| = |u| + |v| can hold and compute the gap in specific cases, Identify equal vectors among arrows drawn at different locations, and identify parallel vectors by factoring scalars. Suggested item count: 12.

Start with story-to-component translation and single magnitudes (developing); move to head-minus-tail vectors between points and componentwise addition with sketches (developing-to-proficient); progress to multi-leg displacement problems, scalar multiples, and return-vector questions (proficient); finish with triangle-inequality analysis and parallelism-by-scalar-factoring (proficient).

**Assessment objectives**

Formats: Multi-leg journey problem: compute net displacement, distance from start, and the return vector, with a supporting diagram, Point-pair items: find the vector from P to Q and its magnitude, with order-of-subtraction accountability, Concept discrimination: given several statements, identify which describe vectors and which describe points or scalars, Numeric counterexample task: exhibit vectors with |u + v| < |u| + |v| and explain the cancellation. Bloom alignment: Apply — students execute vector arithmetic in modeling contexts (journeys, forces) and convert between geometric and component representations; discrimination and counterexample items reach analysis by probing the vector/point distinction and the failure of magnitude additivity..

Mastery signal: The student moves fluently between arrow pictures, displacement stories, and component arithmetic; always computes head minus tail without order errors; predicts correctly when magnitudes add and when they don't; and explains the vector/point distinction with the 'journey vs. place' framing in their own words.

**Teacher notes**

Lead coordinate-free: a full activity of journeys, arrows, and 'same journey, different starting point' before any (a, b) appears, so components arrive as bookkeeping for an idea students already own. Hammer the point/vector distinction early and often — the phrase 'a place versus a journey' resolves most confusions — and require head-minus-tail to be spoken aloud when computing vectors between points. Use the hiker example's 6.32-vs-7.24 km gap as your permanent counterexample to magnitude additivity; students should be able to reproduce it from memory.

**Student notes**

A vector is a journey — how far and which way — not a place: (3, 4) means 'go 3 across and 4 up' from wherever you are, and its length is √(3² + 4²) = 5. To add vectors, chain the journeys head-to-tail or just add matching components; but never add magnitudes unless the directions are identical.

**Prerequisite graph**

- Requires: math.geom.coordinate-plane, math.arith.addition
- Unlocks (future prerequisite links): math.geom.vectors-3d, math.linalg.vector
- Cross-topic connections (graph cross-links): math.linalg.vector
- Narrative: This concept extends directly to math.geom.vectors-3d (the same displacement logic with a third component) and grounds math.linalg.vector, where the add-and-scale arithmetic learned here becomes the axioms of a vector space. Translation vectors from transformation geometry are the same objects, and physics uses this exact machinery for velocity, force, and acceleration.

**Teaching hints — review triggers**

- Student misplots points or confuses axes and signs — review math.geom.coordinate-plane before component work.
- Student makes signed-arithmetic errors like 3 + (−1) or 4 − (−2) — remediate integer addition/subtraction from math.arith.addition, since componentwise operations depend on it.
- Student cannot apply the Pythagorean theorem to find a hypotenuse — review it before magnitude computations.
- Student conflates a translation with its vector or has not seen transformations — a brief revisit of translation (math.geom.translation) provides the natural on-ramp to displacement thinking.

**Spaced repetition / revision guidance**

Redo the hiker computation from scratch: two legs, net displacement, magnitude, and the comparison of straight-line distance versus path length — the whole concept lives inside that one example. Then drill the two highest-error skills separately: five head-minus-tail computations with negative coordinates, and three magnitude calculations with the square root written before any numbers.

---

### Vectors in 3D

*Concept ID: `math.geom.vectors-3d` · Difficulty: proficient · Bloom level: apply · Mastery threshold: 0.8 · Estimated study time: 6h*

**Learning objective.** Students will be able to extend vector reasoning from the plane to space: represent 3D vectors as triples (a, b, c) in the right-handed x-y-z coordinate system, add, subtract, and scale them componentwise, compute magnitudes with the 3D distance formula |v| = √(a² + b² + c²), find vectors between points in space, and work with the standard basis vectors i, j, k.

Vectors in three-dimensional space; introduced by extending the 2D framework to triples (a, b, c) with dot and cross products.

Vectors in 3D are the payoff of learning 2D vectors properly: almost nothing new needs to be believed — only extended. A vector is still a displacement with magnitude and direction; the only change is that journeys can now also go up and down. We append a third coordinate axis, the z-axis, perpendicular to both x and y, oriented by the right-hand rule (point your fingers along +x, curl toward +y, and your thumb gives +z) — a convention chosen once so that everyone's coordinate systems agree. A vector becomes a triple (a, b, c): a units in the x-direction, b in the y-direction, c in the z-direction.
